responses.strategic_rationale: The Bonn residential parasomnia plan sits at the intersection of high-fidelity physiological monitoring, ethically constrained data sharing, and the need for scalable, publishable methods validation. Core tensions include maximizing data richness within safe, cost-conscious operations; maintaining naturalistic observation while ensuring participant safety; and achieving reproducibility and external validation without compromising privacy. The six levers address data fidelity, privacy posture, recruitment efficiency, staffing resilience, technology interoperability, and funding governance, forming a coherent strategy for a sustainable, expandable research unit focused on phenotyping and semi-automated event triage without premature diagnostic claims.

levers.name: Residential capture fidelity and escalation policy
levers.consequences: Expanding the escalation policy to more nights improves event capture and within-subject phenotyping by increasing data richness; downstream, it elevates overnight staffing requirements, participant burden, and data processing workload, potentially slowing turnaround times.
options: Maintain the current tiered monitoring with baseline nights, mid-stay PSG, and escalation nights only when flagged by sensor streams.
Expand escalation to universal PSG across all nights for all participants to maximize data fidelity and event capture.
Adopt an adaptive escalation protocol where only participants with low-quality data or high suspicion of events receive additional PSG.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: data richness versus resource intensity. The options miss explicit escalation-criteria calibration and safeguards for participant burden during extended stays.

levers.name: Data governance and privacy posture for resident data
levers.consequences: Tight privacy controls reduce breach risk and improve compliance, but may limit external validation and cross-site benchmarking; downstream, this can slow replication studies and external collaborations.
options: Enforce a strict privacy-first policy by de-identifying all streams and withholding bedroom video from repository access while sharing derived metrics.
Create a tiered data access framework granting external researchers controlled access under data use agreements with an audit trail.
Deploy federated analysis where raw data remains on-site and only aggregated features are shared for external validation.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: privacy protection versus data-sharing utility. The options overlook potential long-term storage costs and alignment overhead with external partners.

levers.name: Recruitment and retention strategy for parasomnia participants
levers.consequences: Expanding recruitment improves representativeness and enrollment speed, aligning with Aim 2 targets. However, it increases screening workload and potential participant burden due to longer stays. Downstream, budget pressure and scheduling complexity rise.
options: Expand pre-admission screening with enhanced collateral history and awake clinical interview to improve yield.
Offer structured support, including travel stipends and on-site accommodations, to improve retention during longer stays.
Engage regional clinicians under formal referral agreements to stabilize flow and reduce recruitment lag.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: recruitment speed versus consent rigor and participant welfare. The options miss explicit safeguards for consent procedures and monitoring participant burden across longer stays.

levers.name: Staffing model and risk management for 8 active suites
levers.consequences: Increasing permanent staff improves safety and data throughput, but raises costs; conversely, lean staffing heightens risk of gaps and missed events; downstream, reliability of scoring and intervention response could be compromised.
options: Institute a dedicated on-site data-science and scoring team to ensure real-time annotation and reliability.
Adopt a rotating on-call schedule with flexible contractors to cover peak nights while keeping fixed core.
Collaborate with a local sleep clinic for shared technician resources under a formal service agreement.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: staffing stability versus cost containment. The options miss a clear escalation protocol during shortages and a defined safety net for critical nights.

levers.name: Technology stack and data interoperability for synchronized streams
levers.consequences: Standardized pipelines improve reproducibility and annotation throughput; however, integrating multiple sensors can cause data compatibility drift and setup delays; downstream, initial configuration cost and maintenance overhead increase.
options: Adopt EEG-BIDS-compatible pipelines with offline processing to ensure reproducibility and auditable workflows.
Develop rule-based and heuristic event triage methods to identify candidate parasomnias without relying on opaque models.
Pilot a modular data ingestion framework that accommodates new sensors without major rework.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: standardization for reproducibility versus flexibility for new sensors. The options may under-address future hardware changes and long-term maintenance.

levers.name: Funding and governance strategy for sustainability
levers.consequences: Diversifying funding reduces risk and enables longer-term work; but governance and reporting requirements from multiple sources can slow progress and create scope drift.
options: Pursue staged DFG and university funding with milestone gates linked to enrollment and data quality.
Establish a pharma partnership with strict publication rights and a 30-day pre-publication review window.
Form a European network of sleep centers to share methods and co-apply for grants to increase reach.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: funding diversity versus coordination burden. The options overlook potential conflicts of interest safeguards and decision-rights alignment across partners.

responses.summary: Add quarterly external data-use audits to Data governance and privacy posture.

responses.strategic_rationale: The project faces a core tension between rapid data collection from a larger residential footprint and the need to maintain participant safety, data integrity, and regulatory compliance. The proposed levers push on capacity, data fidelity, adjudication efficiency, recruitment breadth, data governance, and safety culture, collectively enabling sustainable pilot execution and scalable expansion. By aligning facility expansion, tiered sensing, streamlined event adjudication, diversified recruitment, robust privacy governance, and proactive safety protocols, the plan can deliver high-quality longitudinal phenotyping while balancing participant burden and staffing constraints. Each lever challenges assumptions about automation, sample completeness, and cost visibility, ensuring decisions remain anchored in risk management and practical delivery within the Bonn setting.

levers.name: Facility capacity expansion gating tied to pilot data quality and staffing readiness
levers.consequences: Expanding capacity increases throughput and accelerates data collection, enabling enrollment targets to be met more quickly. The trade-off is higher capital expenditure and more complex safety oversight, which could delay other program improvements if budgets tighten.
options: Proceed with phased expansion after achieving predefined data quality metrics and staffing safety ratios through pilot outcomes.
Hold expansion in reserve and optimize existing suites' utilization via scheduling and throughput improvements over three quarters.
Scale expansion only after securing additional staffing contracts and ensuring full regulatory compliance for new capacity.
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the tension between expansion and safety readiness during the pilot. The options overlook transition costs and the lead times required to train and certify additional staff.

levers.name: Tiered sensing strategy optimization for data fidelity and participant comfort
levers.consequences: Adopting a tiered sensing approach improves data fidelity during enhanced nights while preserving naturalistic behavior during low-burden monitoring. The trade-off is increased data management complexity and the need for explicit protocols to prevent misalignment between sensor streams.
options: Adopt a strictly gated tiering policy that escalates to full PSG only after a predefined event threshold is met in a given participant stay.
Maintain continuous low-burden monitoring for all nights and reserve enhanced nights for the mid-point and final night of each stay.
Introduce a consent-forward adaptive pipeline that allows participants to opt into additional sensors during selected nights with explicit privacy controls.
levers.review_lever: This lever frames data fidelity versus participant burden, but the options miss the risk of data integration gaps across streams. A fuller view should address cross-stream reconciliation and latency issues.

levers.name: Event triage and adjudication workflow modernization
levers.consequences: Improving adjudication workflow reduces manual review time and speeds the publication cycle, while enhancing consistency across scorers. It also requires robust training and governance to avoid unexpected biases or over-reliance on automation.
options: Implement dual-human blinded scoring with algorithmic triage to prioritize likely parasomnia events for rapid adjudication.
Pilot a staged workflow where junior technicians flag potential events for senior review during overnight shifts.
Rearchitect the event annotation interface to support rapid cross-validation and audit trails across scorers in real-time operations.
levers.review_lever: This lever targets adjudication workflow; the options address process efficiency but may overlook scorer fatigue and inter-rater consistency under long-night shifts. Strengthening cross-scorer calibration would help ensure reliability beyond individual workflows.

levers.name: Recruitment channels diversification and enrollment pacing
levers.consequences: Diversifying recruitment channels expands reach and improves sample diversity, increasing likelihood of meeting enrollment targets. The trade-off is higher administrative overhead and consent management, which could slow decision cycles if not coordinated.
options: Establish regional partnerships with sleep clinics to streamline referrals and reduce pre-admission barriers for eligible participants.
Implement rolling recruitment with flexible eligibility criteria and extended stay windows to counter slow enrollment.
Offer enhanced participant engagement through structured pre-admission interviews and dedicated care coordinators to boost retention.
levers.review_lever: This lever covers recruitment pace versus sample diversity; the options miss risk of selection bias and regulatory complexity. Provisions for monitoring recruitment representativeness and consent load will help avoid skew.

levers.name: Local data governance and privacy alignment with regulatory context
levers.consequences: Strengthening privacy and governance reduces risk of data misuse and regulatory concerns, enabling responsible collaboration. However, it can slow data sharing and external partnerships if approvals are lengthy or opaque.
options: Establish a privacy-by-design data pipeline with strict access controls and de-identified data processing standards.
Negotiate data-use agreements with partner institutions that balance openness and participant protection while sustaining collaboration.
Institute a tiered data-sharing model that permits de-identified datasets to external researchers under robust governance.
levers.review_lever: This lever addresses data governance and consent management complexities with cross-border data sharing, but the options address privacy while not fully anticipating approvals timelines. A clearer governance pathway could reduce delays and misalignment with university policies.

levers.name: Safety culture and incident response readiness for residential settings
levers.consequences: Heightened safety readiness improves incident handling and reduces risk, supporting a stable residential observation environment. The downside is potential strictness that can affect participant comfort and naturalistic behavior in daily routines.
options: Develop proactive incident response drills and safety audits with cross-functional teams and scheduled reviews.
Create a permit-based access policy for non-clinical staff during high-occupancy periods to minimize risk.
Institute continuous safety training and feedback loops to adjust environment and protocols based on incident insights.
levers.review_lever: This lever emphasizes safety culture and incident readiness; the options address response but may impose burdens that affect participant comfort. Explicitly balancing safety with participant autonomy would help maintain naturalistic observation.

responses.summary: Add 'formal regional clinic alliances to streamline referrals and reduce pre-admission friction' to Recruitment channels diversification.

responses.strategic_rationale: The Bonn unit sits at the intersection of safe nocturnal monitoring, privacy-compliant data handling, efficient event adjudication, and timely participant recruitment within a fixed 8-suite footprint. The proposed levers target six decision points: how to staff for reliability without excessive cost, how to govern privacy without choking collaboration, how to validate and scale semi-automated triage, how to broaden recruitment responsibly, how to share data for reproducibility while protecting participants, and how to design a facility that can scale if data quality milestones are met. Together they balance speed, safety, data integrity, and resource discipline in a high-uncertainty, early-stage clinical research setting.

levers.name: Adaptive staffing model for night coverage and cross-training
levers.consequences: Direct effect: Flexible, cross-trained staffing reduces single-point coverage risk and improves safety during peak event windows. Downstream implications include higher initial training costs, more complex scheduling, and potential overtime management as teams adapt to variable event density.
options: Reorganize the night shift into cross-trained teams that cover eight suites with overlapping standby capacity to reduce response latency and maintain safety margins.
Implement a flexible on-call roster aligned with observed event density to optimize staffing costs while preserving patient safety.
Introduce a structured fatigue-management program including micro-breaks and rotation rules to sustain data quality over extended stays.
levers.review_lever: This lever wrestles with coverage continuity versus staff fatigue and training overhead, but the options miss transition costs. The proliferation of cross-trained roles could complicate performance evaluation and retention strategies.

levers.name: Privacy-by-design operational framework for data flows
levers.consequences: Direct effect: Strengthens privacy and compliance posture by restricting access and minimizing identifiable data exposure. Downstream implications include potential delays in data availability for collaborative analyses and added governance overhead for access controls and audits.
options: Enforce strict role-based access and audit trails across all sensing streams, including local de-identification before data transfer.
Implement on-site data processing with local anonymization and minimize cloud transfers to university storage.
Publish a de-identified data-sharing policy that excludes video footage while enabling waveform and sensor-data analyses.
levers.review_lever: This lever balances privacy with data utility, but options overlook potential access delays and governance overhead that could hinder collaboration. Excessive de-identification steps may reduce analytic richness and slow data circulation.

levers.name: Semi-automated event triage governance and validation plan
levers.consequences: Direct effect: Establishes reliability of triage tools and reduces manual review burden; however, calibration may require substantial initial effort and ongoing maintenance to avoid drift. Downstream effects include potential bias in early deployments and the need for ongoing quality metrics.
options: Establish a dual-review workflow where algorithmic scoring is calibrated against expert raters on a rolling sample to maintain alignment.
Institute continuous monitoring of false positives with monthly threshold adjustments and performance reviews to prevent drift.
Pilot a tiered alert system that escalates only high-confidence events to technicians to reduce fatigue and improve response times.
levers.review_lever: This lever targets tooling governance and reviewer workload, but options overlook calibration drift risks and the time needed for robust validation. Early deployment may face under-specified performance criteria and bias in sample selection.

levers.name: Community engagement and recruitment pathway diversification beyond medical networks
levers.consequences: Direct effect: Expands recruitment pool and improves enrollment velocity, but consent workflows may become more complex and data quality may vary with outreach channel. Downstream implications include the need for additional onboarding processes and governance around multi-channel recruitment.
options: Partner with regional sleep clinics and patient advocacy groups to co-create screening criteria and streamline referrals to the Bonn unit.
Offer travel stipends and flexible scheduling to reduce participation barriers and improve retention across longer stays.
Implement pre-screening teleconsultations and online intake forms to triage suitability before admission and optimize site capacity.
levers.review_lever: This lever broadens recruitment channels but risks consent complexity and variable data quality across sites; options miss standardized onboarding. Coordination across groups may require additional governance and monitoring.

levers.name: Expanded data sharing and publication pipeline policy
levers.consequences: Direct effect: Enables external validation and broader scientific impact while preserving privacy controls; may extend publication timelines and require governance reviews. Downstream effects include the need for formal data-use agreements and clear responsibility for data stewardship.
options: Create data-use agreements that enable external researchers to access de-identified EEG-BIDS datasets under strict access controls.
Establish a data embargo schedule that balances publication priority with broader verification efforts by different groups.
Coordinate with regional hospitals to develop a federated data sharing framework that avoids central data aggregation while enhancing reproducibility.
levers.review_lever: This lever expands external validation but could slow publication cadence if governance reviews become bottlenecks. Clear SLAs and defined review scope are needed to prevent delays.

levers.name: Facility design for scalable modular expansion
levers.consequences: Direct effect: Keeps capital expenditure aligned with data quality milestones and allows rapid scaling, but adds planning and modular integration risk. Downstream implications include potential disruption during expansion and the need for future-proofed IT and safety integration.
options: Adopt a modular renovation plan with plug-in suite modules that can be added without disrupting ongoing nightly monitoring.
Create a flexible staging area for equipment and supplies that decouples renovation work from live monitoring operations.
Develop a robust power and network architecture that supports rapid expansion contingent on data quality milestones.
levers.review_lever: This lever enables scaling but introduces modular integration risk and potential architectural debt; options miss phased cost control. A staged road map with compatibility checks is essential.

responses.summary: Add 'pilot community outreach partnerships with Bonn regional sleep clinics to accelerate recruitment' to Community engagement and recruitment pathway diversification lever.

system_prompt:
You are an expert strategic analyst. Generate solution space parameters following these directives:

1. **Output Requirements**
   - You must generate 5 to 7 levers per response.
   - Each lever's `options` field must contain exactly 3 qualitative strategic choices as plain strings.

2. **Lever Quality Standards**
   - Consequences: describe the direct effect of pulling this lever, then at least one downstream implication or trade-off. Be concise and grounded — only cite specific numbers if the project context provides evidence for them. Do not fabricate percentages or cost estimates. Target length: 2–4 sentences.
   - Options MUST:
     • Represent genuinely distinct strategic pathways (not just labels)
     • Include at least one unconventional or non-obvious approach
     • NO prefixes (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")

3. **Strategic Framing**
   - Name each lever using language drawn directly from the project's own domain — avoid formulaic patterns or repeated prefixes
   - Frame options as complete strategic approaches
   - Ensure levers challenge core project assumptions

4. **Validation Protocols**
   - For `review_lever`:
     A short critical review — name the core tension, then identify a weakness the options miss.
     Examples:
     - "This lever governs the tension between centralization and local autonomy, but the options overlook transition costs."
     - "Prioritizing speed over reliability carries hidden costs: none of the options address rollback complexity."
     Do not use square brackets or placeholder text.
   - For `summary`:
     One sentence prescribing a concrete addition to a specific lever.
     Example: "Add 'partner with a regional distributor for last-mile logistics' to Supply Chain Strategy."

5. **Prohibitions**
   - NO prefixes/labels in options (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")
   - NO generic option labels (e.g., "Optimize X", "Tolerate Y")
   - NO placeholder consequences or bracket-wrapped templates
   - NO fabricated statistics or percentages without evidence from the project context
   - NO marketing language (e.g., "game-changing", "cutting-edge", "revolutionary")

6. **Option Structure**
   - Maintain parallel grammatical structure across options
   - Ensure options are self-contained descriptions
   - Each option should be a concrete, actionable approach — not a vague aspiration
user_prompt:
File 'plan.txt':
Plan:
Establish a 3-year residential longitudinal research unit in Bonn, Germany, for the study of adult NREM parasomnias — sleepwalking, confusional arousals, and sleep terrors — with a secondary exploratory arm for REM sleep behavior disorder under a separate protocol with dedicated neurologic screening, reflecting RBD's distinct clinical trajectory per current AASM guidance. The facility is affiliated with University Hospital Bonn's Department of Epileptology, leveraging its existing long-term video-EEG monitoring culture, and addresses a specific methodological gap: traditional single-night polysomnography rarely captures infrequent parasomnia events, while outpatient actigraphy lacks physiological resolution. The unit houses 8 active sleep suites at launch in a converted residential property in a quiet Bonn neighborhood, renovated for safety (padded corridor edges, restricted-opening windows, silent exterior door alarms, fire safety, acoustic treatment, network cabling, accessibility modifications) while preserving a domestic feel — if the environment is too clinical, naturalistic behavior is suppressed. Physical capacity exists for 12 suites, but expansion is deferred until the pilot proves acceptable data quality, manageable false alarm rates, safe staffing ratios, and usable annotation throughput. Sensing uses a tiered acquisition model: nightly low-burden monitoring via dry-electrode EEG headbands, contact-free mattress sensors, unobtrusively mounted infrared cameras, ambient microphones, and environmental sensors; scheduled enhanced nights with fuller PSG montage including standard scalp EEG, EOG, chin EMG, and leg EMG on the first night, one mid-stay night, and the final night per participant; and escalation nights deploying full PSG after technician-flagged events to capture recurrence at clinical-grade resolution. All streams are time-synchronized and stored in EEG-BIDS-compatible format on a local NAS with nightly encrypted backup to university storage, annotated in real time by the night technician and later scored by two independent raters using AASM criteria with inter-rater reliability reported. Public data sharing is limited to de-identified physiological and sensor data under data use agreements — bedroom video carries severe privacy constraints and is not deposited in public repositories. The scientific program has three explicit aims: Aim 1, establish and operationally validate the residential capture model as safe, ethics-approved, and sustainable; Aim 2, characterize within-person and between-person parasomnia patterns longitudinally, including episode frequency, morphology, triggers, and temporal clustering; Aim 3, develop and benchmark semi-automated event-triage tools that rank probable parasomnia episodes and reduce manual review burden, evaluated against dual human scorer agreement rather than promising autonomous diagnosis.

Participant recruitment targets adults aged 18–65 with NREM parasomnia confirmed through a structured pre-admission pathway: specialist clinical interview, collateral history from bed partner or household member where available, prior or screening polysomnography, and a structured exclusion checklist adjudicated by the PI. Explicit exclusions are nocturnal epilepsy or suspected epilepsy mimics, untreated obstructive sleep apnea, active major substance use disorder, severe psychiatric instability, and wandering or injury risk beyond facility capability. Recruitment channels are the University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic, regional neurologist referrals, and the Deutsche Gesellschaft für Schlafforschung und Schlafmedizin network, with per-night compensation of €80 plus meals. A matched control group provides baseline nocturnal movement, arousal patterns, and false-positive sensor activity under identical residential monitoring conditions, enabling benchmarking of the event-triage tools against normal nocturnal behavior. Event-yield planning assumes that based on the inclusion criterion of at least 2 self-reported episodes per month, approximately 70–80% of admitted participants will produce at least one captured and adjudicated event during a 2–8 week stay; stays may be extended up to 2 additional weeks for participants with zero captured events after the initial period, capped at 10 weeks total, with a protocol ceiling of 20% unproductive admissions before triggering a recruitment-criteria review. The core team is 9 people: a PI who is a board-certified sleep medicine physician, 2 postdoctoral researchers in sleep neurophysiology and computational neuroscience respectively, 3 research technicians rotating night shifts to ensure safe coverage of 8 active suites with adequate margin for event response and annotation, a clinical psychologist specializing in sleep disorders, a data engineer managing the sensor pipeline, and a study coordinator handling recruitment, consent, scheduling, and ethics compliance.

Budget is €3.8 million over 3 years, gated: €1.8M year one covering property lease and renovation at approximately €750K, equipment at approximately €450K, staff hiring accounting for German social insurance contributions and actual overnight coverage, ethics approval, and a pilot cohort of 15–20 participants; €1.2M year two covering full operations with 30–35 participants, the enhanced-night PSG program, first publications, and event-triage algorithm development; €800K year three conditional on demonstrable progress by month 24. Personnel costs are approximately €2M over three years and remaining funds cover participant compensation, independent scoring time, data storage and retention, regulatory and legal work, and contingency. Funding sources are DFG research grant and University of Bonn internal research funding as the primary pillars, with a pharmaceutical industry partnership as optional upside rather than a feasibility requirement — if secured, the academic team retains full publication rights with the pharma partner receiving a 30-day pre-publication comment window only. Success criteria at 36 months: safe residential monitoring operations sustained for at least 24 months, enrollment of 50–70 NREM parasomnia participants plus matched controls, capture of a prespecified minimum number of independently adjudicated parasomnia events sufficient for Aim 2 phenotyping analysis, 2–3 peer-reviewed publications in sleep medicine journals covering methods validation and longitudinal characterization, a benchmarked semi-automated event-triage model with reported sensitivity, specificity, and agreement against dual human scoring, de-identified physiological dataset deposited in a recognized repository, and a submitted phase-two grant proposal for expansion and intervention studies. Failure triggers: if by month 18 the facility has enrolled fewer than 25 participants or captured fewer than 40 adjudicated events, the program undergoes external review. Governance is a 3-member external scientific advisory board reviewing progress every 6 months, with the PI holding operational authority and budget reallocations above €50K requiring board approval. Banned words: AI, blockchain, app, VR, AR, gamification, wearable startup, consumer product, DAO, NFT, metaverse, smart home, autonomous diagnosis, digital therapeutic, SaaS, marketplace, validated (in the context of claiming a mature deployable system). Pick a realistic scenario — this is an early-stage clinical research facility producing methods validation and phenotyping work, not a finished diagnostic platform.

**Explanation:** This plan *unequivocally requires* a physical location (the research unit in Bonn, Germany), physical renovations, physical equipment, in-person staffing, and physical participants. The entire research project *revolves around* physical data collection and analysis in a real-world setting. The plan also requires physical travel to the location.
